does: This paper introduces SAGE, a scalable ground truth evaluation framework for sparse autoencoders that automatically identifies task-specific activations and computes ground truth features without requiring extensive manual circuit analysis. The method addresses the core limitation that SAEs currently lack gold-standard evaluation metrics by creating synthetic ground truth labels at model activations relevant to specific tasks. Rather than relying on toy models with predefined features or manual circuit knowledge, SAGE generates ground truth data by leveraging task structure itself, enabling evaluation that scales to large modern SAEs and realistic neural network architectures while substantially reducing computational overhead compared to prior approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of sparse autoencoders as mechanistic interpretability tools, extending recent progress in decomposing neural network activations into interpretable features. It directly addresses a critical bottleneck identified in SAE research: the lack of principled evaluation metrics beyond toy domains. The paper engages with prior work on circuit analysis and causal intervention methods that require extensive domain knowledge, while also drawing on the broader interpretability literature's attempts to validate feature decompositions. SAGE represents a natural evolution from proxy-based evaluation methods toward more scalable, automated approaches that work on realistic models and tasks.
enables: By enabling systematic evaluation of SAEs on large models, this framework opens pathways for more reliable feature discovery across diverse architectures and tasks. The scalable ground truth mechanism could support automated hyperparameter optimization for SAE training and help identify which sparse autoencoder designs best capture behaviorally-relevant features. Future work could leverage these evaluation tools to improve SAE design, scale interpretability audits to production language models, and build more sophisticated feature analysis pipelines that combine ground truth evaluation with causal methods. The framework also potentially enables researchers to more rigorously compare different mechanistic interpretability approaches beyond SAEs.
